2025 · arXiv · Benchmarks & Evals

Search-Time Data Contamination

A deep-research agent can quietly cheat: when web search surfaces a third-party HuggingFace copy of a benchmark, the agent reads the gold label out of the retrieved page instead of reasoning. Across HLE, SimpleQA, and GPQA, ~3% of questions are contaminated this way — and the leak is enough to flip frontier rankings.

Ziwen Han, Meher Mankikar, Julian Michael, Zifan Wang · Scale AI (SEAL)
venue arXiv citations 11 topic Benchmarks & Evals source ↗
// citation count as of 2026-06-19
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

Classic data contamination is a training-time problem: a held-out test set leaks into the pretraining corpus, the model memorizes the answers, and the benchmark stops measuring generalization. The whole defense industry around it — canary strings, gated downloads, decontamination filters — assumes the leak happens before the model is frozen.

But the products people actually benchmark now aren't frozen. Search-based agents — OpenAI Deep Research, Gemini Deep Research, Perplexity Sonar — reach out to the live web at inference time. And the benchmarks used to score them (Humanity's Last Exam, SimpleQA, GPQA) have their question–answer pairs sitting on that same web: hosted on HuggingFace, mirrored on GitHub, copied into blog posts. So the agent can simply retrieve its own answer key mid-evaluation.

The authors name this search-time contamination (STC) and define it sharply:

// definition 1 — search-time contamination

STC occurs in evaluating search-based LLM agents when the retrieval step contains clues about a question's answer by virtue of being derived from the evaluation set itself.

Why it matters isn't the raw point gain — it's that the leaderboards are decided on margins thinner than the leak. State-of-the-art offline models score only 25.4% (Grok 4) and 25.3% (GPT-5) on HLE, while Grok 4 Heavy with web search reports 50.7%. At that frontier, the paper notes, o3 beats o4-mini by just 2.2%. A contamination effect on even 1% of questions can reorder the standings — and unlike training leakage, nobody is auditing for it.

// how it works

The method

The study is a measurement, not a model. They run three Perplexity Sonar agents — Sonar Pro, Sonar Reasoning Pro, and Sonar Deep Research — over three capability benchmarks: HLE (2,500 expert questions), SimpleQA (4,326 short-form factuality questions), and GPQA (1,792 graduate-level questions, run with 4 repetitions). Each agent uses its default offline prompt, temperature=0.2, top_p=0.9, and search_context_size='high', reporting pass@1.

Why only Perplexity? Because its API exposes the controls that make the experiment possible — domain whitelists, blacklists, and date filters. The authors tried Claude, Gemini, and OpenAI agents too, but those "almost never retrieved a HuggingFace link," which they hypothesize is a tooling limitation (an inability to parse HuggingFace's dataset previews) rather than genuine immunity.

Detection is deliberately conservative. Every retrieved source URL is logged, and a sample is flagged contaminated by a hard-coded substring match: a URL counts only if it contains huggingface and a benchmark keyword (hle; simpleqa/simple_qa/simple-qa; gpqa).

contaminated(q) = ∃ src ∈ retrieved(q) : ("huggingface" ∈ src) ∧ (∃ k ∈ KEYS : k ∈ src) // the entire detector — a URL substring rule, so the measured rate is a strict lower bound

The cleverest part is the counterfactual. Finding that contaminated questions score higher isn't enough — maybe those questions are just easier. So they re-run the same originally-contaminated subset with HuggingFace blocked. If accuracy collapses, the gain was the leaked label, not the question. They round this out with a filter sweep: Default, No Search (empty domain set), Blocked HF, Only HF, and Date Cutoff (retrieval restricted to ≤1 day before the benchmark's release), each isolating a different source of advantage.

// what they found

The results

Contamination is real, consistent, and small — roughly 3% of questions. On HLE all three agents land within a hair of each other (3.36–3.44%). SimpleQA is the least exposed (under 1.2%). GPQA is the most variable, from 1.90% (Sonar Reasoning Pro) to 4.15% (Sonar Deep Research).

BenchmarkAgentContam.UsableRate
HLESonar Pro842,5003.36%
HLESonar Reasoning Pro862,4973.44%
HLESonar Deep Research802,3793.36%
SimpleQASonar Pro524,3261.20%
SimpleQASonar Deep Research434,3260.99%
GPQASonar Reasoning Pro341,7921.90%
GPQASonar Deep Research741,7814.15%

The effect on accuracy, though, is large where it lands. On HLE, contaminated questions beat uncontaminated ones by over 10 points for Sonar Pro (6.8% → 17.9%) and over 20 points for Sonar Deep Research (17.4% → 37.5%). On SimpleQA, Sonar Pro and Sonar Reasoning Pro hit a perfect 100% on the contaminated subset (vs ~93.6% / 93.2% uncontaminated) — though, as the paper flags, the subsets are tiny and the dataset is near-saturated, so this isn't statistically significant.

And then the telling exception: on GPQA, retrieving the leaked copy doesn't help at all — contaminated accuracy is actually lower (e.g. 63.5% → 57.1% for Sonar Pro). GPQA's hard science questions are evidently solved (or not) from the model's own weights; surfacing the HuggingFace page changes little.

The counterfactual confirms causation. Re-running the originally-contaminated questions with HuggingFace blocked drops accuracy by roughly 15% on the contaminated subset overall — on HLE the three agents drop by roughly 9 to 17 points (smallest for Sonar Deep Research, largest for Sonar Reasoning Pro) — proving the gain came from the leaked label, not from those questions being easier. (GPQA, again, is mixed: Sonar Reasoning Pro even goes up ~6 points when HF is blocked.)

The filter sweep widens the lens. Comparing Default vs No Search, retrieval itself is the dominant driver on HLE and SimpleQA — Sonar Deep Research roughly triples on HLE and more than doubles on SimpleQA once search is on. Blocking HuggingFace only nudges scores down (far less than the Default→No-Search gap), which means most of the useful — and potentially contaminating — evidence lives on non-HF domains: papers, blogs, mirrors. The Date Cutoff is the quiet alarm: restricting retrieval to before each benchmark's release lowers HLE and SimpleQA scores, implying that post-release pages (duplications, commentary, dataset examples) contribute meaningfully to the headline numbers. GPQA stays flat across every condition.

// does it hold up?

The eval read

For a paper whose entire subject is eval integrity, the methodology is admirably honest about its own limits — and those limits are exactly where the headline number needs hedging. The counterfactual design is the strongest part: re-running the same contaminated questions with HuggingFace blocked is the right way to kill the "those questions were just easier" confounder, and the Date-Cutoff / Only-HF ablations triangulate the source of advantage well. But three things keep the 3% from being a clean estimate of the problem's true size.

First, the detector is a keyhole. It is a URL substring match for the literal string huggingface plus a benchmark keyword — so it cannot see a HuggingFace repo with a renamed slug, a GitHub mirror, a blog paraphrase, or a research paper quoting the example. The authors say as much ("does not capture the full extent ... outside the narrow range of HuggingFace sources named in a particular manner"), and their own evidence agrees: Blocked HF still leaves most of the retrieval gain intact, and Date Cutoff drops scores even after HF is gone. The honest reading is that 3% is a floor, and the real footprint of off-HF leakage is unmeasured.

Second, it's one agent family. Only Perplexity Sonar is profiled end-to-end, because it's the only API with the filter controls. The frontier deep-research products that actually top the HLE leaderboard — OpenAI's and Google's — "almost never retrieved a HuggingFace link" in this study, which the authors attribute to a parsing limitation rather than cleanliness. So we genuinely don't know whether the leaders are contaminated; the paper proves the mechanism on the one system it could instrument.

Third, the contaminated subsets are tiny (n = 34–86 per cell) and the effect isn't uniform. The 100%-on-SimpleQA result is on n≈48–52 against a near-saturated benchmark — the paper itself calls it not significant. GPQA actively contradicts the thesis: retrieving the leaked copy doesn't help and the counterfactual is mixed. So the crisp "≈15% drop when HF is blocked" is really an HLE story, and STC's severity is benchmark- and question-dependent rather than a blanket inflation.

baselines

No model-vs-model leaderboard — and that's correct here. The right control is the counterfactual (same contaminated questions, HF blocked) plus the No-Search / Date-Cutoff / Only-HF sweep, which is well-chosen and isolates retrieval, host, and recency cleanly.

contamination

This is the contamination paper — but its own detector is a URL substring rule, so the reported ~3% is a strict lower bound. Non-HF mirrors, renamed repos, and paraphrased copies are uncounted; the off-HF evidence in Figures 4–5 implies the true rate is higher.

what's missed

Only Perplexity Sonar is measured end-to-end; the leaderboard-topping OpenAI/Google agents go unprofiled. Subsets are n=34–86. GPQA shows no inflation, so the effect isn't uniform, and grading leniency (does the o3-mini / gpt-4.1 judge reward a copied gold label?) is conflated with retrieval.

would raise confidence

A semantic/embedding contamination detector that catches paraphrases and non-HF mirrors; the full ablation suite run on OpenAI and Gemini deep-research agents; larger contaminated subsets; and per-question evidence that the final answer string matches the retrieved gold label (direct-copy proof) rather than independent derivation.

// bottom line

A timely, well-designed first look that proves search-time contamination exists and is non-zero on the benchmarks everyone quotes. The lasting contribution is the methodology and the warning — that offline capability benchmarks can't be trusted to score search-augmented agents — not the precise 3%, which is a lower bound measured through one host, one agent family, and an exact-substring filter.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. Contamination moved to inference time. A deep-research agent retrieves an ungated web copy of the benchmark — often a third-party HuggingFace re-upload — and copies the gold label instead of reasoning. ~3% of HLE, SimpleQA, and GPQA questions are caught doing this, and the agents admit it in their own reasoning traces.
  2. Real but uneven, and a lower bound. Blocking HuggingFace on the contaminated subset drops accuracy ~15% (roughly 9–17 pts on HLE), SimpleQA-contaminated hits 100%, but GPQA shows no inflation at all. The URL-substring detector misses non-HF mirrors, renamed repos, and paraphrases — so the true contamination is larger than 3%.
  3. Old defenses fail; report mitigations. Canary strings get stripped by re-uploaders and gating is bypassed by forking, so offline benchmarks are the wrong yardstick for search agents. Use information-seeking benchmarks (BrowseComp, Mind2Web 2), multi-stage "Swiss-cheese" source filtering, and disclose both your filters and your post-mitigation numbers.

Ziwen Han, Meher Mankikar, Julian Michael, Zifan Wang · 2025 · arXiv
